modules.title: Answering past-paper comprehension questions
modules.html:
SSS 3 comprehension is examination-style. Question types include:

  • Factual — the answer is stated in the passage. Copy or paraphrase.
  • Inference — you must deduce from clues.
  • Vocabulary in context — choose the meaning that fits the passage.
  • Grammatical name / function — name a phrase or clause and give its function (e.g. 'noun clause acting as object of the verb said').
  • Author's purpose / tone — identify why the writer wrote and how they feel.
  • Figures of speech — identify and briefly explain.

Answering strategy

  1. Read the question first; then read the passage twice.
  2. Underline the relevant words in the passage.
  3. Answer in complete sentences; do not lift long sections verbatim unless asked.

examples.q: How would you answer a 'grammatical name and function' question?
examples.a: Name the type of construction (e.g. adverbial clause of reason), then state what it modifies (e.g. modifies the verb stayed).

modules.title: Complex summary tasks and word limits
modules.html:
SSS 3 summaries carry strict rules. Follow the exact number and type of points asked. Stay within the word limit.

Method

  1. Read the passage twice; underline the ideas that answer the question.
  2. Group related ideas; drop repetitions and examples.
  3. Rewrite in your own words as short, clear sentences.
  4. Count the words; trim redundancies (each and every → each, due to the fact that → because).
  5. Never add opinion; never introduce information not in the passage.

Answer exactly the number of points required. Extras may be ignored or, worse, penalised.

examples.q: If a question asks for three reasons the writer opposes gambling and you list five, what happens?
examples.a: Examiners usually mark only the first three; extras waste time and can dilute quality.

modules.title: Detecting tone, purpose and figurative devices
modules.html:
Tone is the writer's attitude — critical, sarcastic, admiring, alarmed, nostalgic, humorous, urgent, sombre.

Purpose — to inform, persuade, entertain, warn, praise.

Watch out for:

  • Irony — words meaning the opposite of what is said.
  • Sarcasm — biting mockery.
  • Rhetorical questions — used for effect.
  • Repetition and parallelism — for emphasis.
  • Symbolism — an object standing for an idea.

examples.q: Identify the device: Ask not what your country can do for you; ask what you can do for your country.
examples.a: Parallelism and antithesis — parallel structure with contrasting halves.

quiz.q: A 'grammatical name' question expects you to state:
options: the type of phrase/clause and its function

quiz.q: If asked for three reasons, you should give:
Word-limit trimming can start by removing:
Due to the fact that can be shortened to:
Which tone signals warning?
Which is a rhetorical device?
Ask not what your country… uses:
Great! I love missing the bus. shows:
A factual comprehension question is best answered by:
Vocabulary-in-context requires you to:
options: pick the meaning that fits the sentence

quiz.q: Which of these is a passage-based inference?
Which is <u>not</u> a valid tone?
quiz.solution: Onomatopoeia is a device, not a tone.

quiz.q: Which is a purpose of writing?
Passed away is a:
Which shows repetition for emphasis?
options: We must fight. We must fight. We must fight.

quiz.q: A sentence with figures of speech in comprehension should be answered by:
Which sentence uses irony?
options: How lovely — my car has broken down again.
quiz.solution: Positive words for a negative event.

quiz.q: A summary of an argumentative passage should list:
An 'author's purpose' question requires:
options: identify why the passage was written

quiz.q: Which is a signal of contrast in a passage?
Grammatical name for who saved the child in The boy who saved the child was rewarded:
Function of the same clause:
Grammatical name for because it was raining:
Function of the same clause in We stayed indoors because it was raining:
Grammatical name for What he said in What he said surprised us:
A summary sentence should be:
A poor summary technique:
Which linker best joins two related summary sentences?
A one-line summary is called a:
Which is <u>not</u> a summary question type?
quiz.solution: Tone is a comprehension question, not a summary task.

quiz.q: Choose the concise equivalent of in view of the fact that:
Concise equivalent of at this point in time:
Concise equivalent of the majority of:
Meaning of succinct:
Meaning of verbose:
An examiner will penalise summaries that:
Which device does She sells sea-shells on the sea-shore employ?
Which device does Life is a stage employ?
Which device does as light as a feather employ?
Which device does the sun peeped through the clouds employ?
Which is <u>not</u> a legitimate summary source?
Best synonym of brief:
Which sentence is most concise?
options: Due to the fact that she was tired, she slept.
Because she was tired, she slept.
In view of tiredness, she slept.
She slept for the reason of tiredness.

quiz.q: Which sentence is passive?
Change to active voice: The prize was won by Ada.
options: Ada was won by the prize.

quiz.q: Which device: Ask not… ask (repeating structure)?
Reading strategy for exam comprehension: first…
Which is a valid comprehension answer style?
Which is a rhetorical question?
options: Isn't it time we said enough is enough?

quiz.q: She entered noiselessly. The word noiselessly is a:
